title: Warhammer Quest (1995) - Still the Gold Standard - Part 1 - Overview and Base Game
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IuZyLH50TMQ

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns to the original Warhammer Quest as the second entry in the Dungeon Dive Master Class series, approaching the game as it exists today—a living, thriving title sustained by decades of fan expansion, particularly the work of Little Monk. He opens with a firm warning: do not pay collector prices. DIY builds using freely available files can look fantastic.

The game earns its gold standard through a survival campaign where emergent narrative trumps scripted story, random dungeons with meaningful exploration choices (corridors split, and you may never reach the objective), a massive bestiary, non-tiered loot (a level-one hero can wield any weapon they find), and a settlement/travel phase that creates a living world between quests. The 200-page Role Play Book transforms the basic board game into something no other boxed product has attempted—adding spells, a comprehensive bestiary, warrior levelling, GM-led adventures, and a troll slayer class. Daniel acknowledges the weaknesses: extreme power creep past level five, tedious combat lacking tactical movement, and a reliance on fan content to reach its full potential. He walks through every component in the box, from Jeff Taylor's legendary cover art to the four warriors and the deceptively deadly giant rats.

----

Is Warhammer Quest's reliance on fan content a strength or a vulnerability for its long-term legacy?
